post: Will you be at ASU+GSV Summit next week? Wed love to connect! Join us Tuesday night for a rooftop afterparty at The Nolen. Well have drinks, snacks, sweets, and a great group, all with one of the best views in San Diego. Well also be sharing an early look at BRIs new Civics Innovation Lab and how were working to transform K–12 civic education. RSVP today:  https://luma.com/8enscyri 📍 The Nolen Rooftop 🗓 Tuesday, April 14 | 8:15–10:00 PM Hope to see you there!

The Supreme Court is weighing one of the most foundational questions in constitutional law. Your students don't have to wait for the ruling to engage it. In Trump v. Barbara, the Court is examining executive power over birthright citizenship: "All persons born or naturalized in the United States, and subject to the jurisdiction thereof, are citizens." 6 words – "subject to the jurisdiction thereof" – are doing a lot of work in that sentence. What do they mean? Who decides? And what principles should guide that interpretation? Think the Vote's latest question: Should all individuals born in the United States automatically receive citizenship? Students will encounter 2 serious constitutional traditions here: jus soli, citizenship by place of birth, upheld by the Supreme Court since U. S. v. Wong Kim Ark in 1898, and the argument that the Fourteenth Amendment's language leaves meaningful room for interpretation. The ability to reason through both positions, rather than react to them, is exactly the civic muscle this moment demands. Bring the Constitution into your classroom while the Court is still deliberating. Student submissions close April 23, share it this week. 👉  https://bit.ly/4tHKf29  #CivicsEducation  #ThinktheVote  #SCOTUS  #FourteenthAmendment  #BirthRightCitizenship  #ConstitutionalLaw  #SocialStudies  #HighSchoolTeachers  #BillofRightsInstitute

#OTD 1913, the 17th Amendment was ratified, and it changed how Americans choose their senators permanently. Before that day, U. S. senators weren't elected by the people. They were chosen by state legislatures. The Founders designed it deliberately as part of the careful architecture of republican government. The Senate was meant to check popular passion. State legislatures were meant to have a direct voice in federal governance, a key feature of federalism. By the early 20th century, critics argued the system had become a vehicle for corruption. Moneyed interests, they said, could effectively purchase Senate seats by controlling state legislators. The Progressive movement pushed hard for reform, and the 17th Amendment was the result: direct, popular election of senators. It's a fascinating case study in constitutional evolution, and in the ongoing American tension between federalism and democratic accountability, between the Founders' design and the people's demand. BRI's lesson on Congress in the 20th and 21st centuries puts this moment in full context for your students 👇  https://bit.ly/4sjHD9m  #TeachHistory  #CivicsEducation  #17thAmendment  #ConstitutionalHistory  #Federalism  #AmericanHistory  #TDiH  #BRI  #AmericaAt250

Theodore Roosevelt had a theory about political extremism. He'd seen it before as a child: fire-eaters in the South, radical abolitionists in the North. And he believed Abraham Lincoln had shown the way forward. Not by splitting the difference, but by becoming what he called "the golden mean." Roosevelt spent his entire political career trying to be that. In this episode of BRI Scholar Talks, historian David Brown, author of In the Arena: Theodore Roosevelt in War, Peace, and Revolution, joins BRIs Tony Williams to explore how TR's sense of civic duty, his belief in the strenuous life, and his willingness to take on his own party transformed the American presidency. Watch the full conversation:  https://bit.ly/4cwKlUh  #AmericanHistory  #CivicEducation  #TeachingHistory  #SocialStudies

Most teachers who share the MyImpact Challenge with their students don't realize there's also something in it for them. The MyImpact Challenge includes a dedicated teacher prize structure. Educators whose students submit valid entries are eligible for recognition, including: - $500 Teacher Grand Prize awarded to the teacher of the national grand prize winner - $500 Teacher Support Prizes for the educators who generate the most entries - $250 Teacher Submission Prize for teachers with 3+ valid submissions from their students The teachers who open this door for their students deserve to be recognized for doing so. The MyImpact Challenge functions best not as an extracurricular add-on, but as a framework that gives structure to the community work students are often already doing. AP Government teachers have used it as an applied civics unit. Service learning coordinators have built whole semesters around it. Guidance counselors have pointed student clubs toward it as a ready-made submission vehicle for projects already underway. In each of those cases, a teacher made it possible. Deadline: May 3. If you have students doing meaningful work in their communities, your next step is straightforward. 👉  https://bit.ly/3fyGGr3  #MyImpactChallenge  #CivicsEducation  #TeacherRecognition  #ServiceLearning  #BillOfRightsInstitute

20 years from now, your students won't remember the test. They'll remember the day the Declaration actually meant something to them, when the words stopped being words and started being ideas worth caring about. The Civic Star Challenge is asking you to capture that moment. Share it, enter the weekly $300 drawing, and let the work happening in your classroom count for more than the grade book. Enter this week:  https://bit.ly/463KXxf  #CivicStarChallenge  #America250  #CivicsTeacher  #TeacherImpact  #BillOfRightsInstitute

#OTD 1968, Martin Luther King Jr. was assassinated in Memphis, Tennessee. He had spent years doing something that history teachers know is harder than it sounds: holding America accountable to its own Founding promises. The Declaration of Independence says all people are created equal, endowed with inalienable rights. King's life's work was a demand that the nation mean it not just as an aspiration, but as an obligation. He didn't deal in abstractions. He marched. He organized. He went to jail. And through it all, he modeled the civic virtues that belong at the center of any serious civics curriculum: courage in the face of real danger, justice as a demand rather than a platitude, humility before the weight of history. At the Bill of Rights Institute, we believe in teaching the whole story of America, including the moments when the nation fell catastrophically short of its ideals. April 4, 1968 is one of those moments. King's legacy – his conviction that the arc of the moral universe bends toward justice – is a lesson in what it looks like to keep believing in a republic when it fails you. Explore BRI's lesson on MLK's dream and identity with your students 👇  https://bit.ly/48dD49n  #TeachHistory  #CivicsEducation  #MLK  #MartinLutherKingJr  #CivilRights  #AmericanHistory  #TDiH  #BillOfRightsInstitute  #AmericaAt250

Since launching Civics Champion last spring, BRI has fulfilled over 50,000 Pocket Constitutions for teachers across the country. That's 50,000 students holding the document that defines their rights. Teachers post what they need. Donors make it happen. The platform connects them directly. Find a civics classroom near you to support, or share this with someone who believes in the next generation of citizens. 👉  https://bit.ly/4coYN0s  #CivicsChampion  #CivicEducation  #Philanthropy  #Constitution

Rosa Parks didn't just want a seat on a bus. She was making a claim on the promises of the Declaration of Independence. BRI's newest A250 Mini Documentary examines the Montgomery Bus Boycott not just as a civil rights milestone but also as the story of ordinary people invoking constitutional principles to demand equal protection under the law. From a single act of defiance to a Supreme Court ruling, this is what civic courage looks like in practice. Watch it here:  https://bit.ly/4m7qAWH  #RosaParks  #AmericanHistory  #CivilRights  #CivicEducation  #TeachingHistory  #SocialStudies  #A250
